content:
The stated goal

Bonnie Haig was a painter with 18 paintings showcased on her website [site audit] and a stated goal of growing her email subscriber list.

The actual problem

The engagement conducted a full audit of the website, Instagram, and social presence [site audit, bonniehaig.com]. What the audit found:

  • Only one reference to “special discounts” anywhere on the site [site audit]
  • No mention of selling on Instagram [Instagram audit]
  • No Facebook business page [social audit]1
  • As an objective visitor: it was not possible to determine whether the site sold anything at all

The reframe

Before answering the email list question, the engagement reframed the task. The question was not how to grow subscribers — it was what the underlying business goal was.

  • Pure audience engagement → one strategy
  • Painting sales → a fundamentally different strategy

The USP recommendation

The central recommendation: build Bonnie’s identity around her nursing professional background.

Benchmark: Leonid Afremov — cited as a standard for emotionally compelling visual art with a recognizable, specific identity [leonidafremov.com]2.

Supporting recommendations

  • Establish a Facebook business page 1
  • Make selling intent explicit on the homepage
  • Build the email capture strategy around the positioned USP

The outcome

Comprehensive marketing strategy assessment delivered [engagement records]. Implementation at the client’s discretion.

Sources

  1. Meta. Facebook Business Pages — Overview. facebook.com/business. Facebook Business Pages enable advertising, audience targeting, and commerce features not available on personal profiles. ↩︎ ↩︎

  2. Afremov, Leonid. leonidafremov.com. Palette-knife oil technique and emotionally charged colour palette — used here as a benchmark for USP clarity in visual art marketing. ↩︎
description: Bonnie Haig wanted to grow her email list. Her site, Instagram, and social presence communicated no commercial intent — only one 'special discounts' reference, no selling visible on Instagram, no Facebook business page. The engagement reframed the goal and recommended building her identity around her nursing background.
title: Bonnie Haig Art: Why a painter with 18 works and a subscriber goal needed a USP before she needed a funnel

content:
The situation

whichkitchenappliance.com was a well-performing UK kitchen appliance affiliate blog. At its pre-collapse peak: 68 keywords in the top 3, 157 in the top 10, and 365 in the top 100 — average position 44.39, estimated daily traffic 72.66 [SEMrush Landscape, Nov 24–30, 2021]1. Google Analytics confirmed 147,325 en-gb users and 168,276 sessions between April 2021 and January 2022 [Google Analytics export, Apr 2021–Jan 2022]2.

On November 28, 2021, organic traffic collapsed sharply. Lisa reached out in February 2022 unable to identify the cause.

The diagnosis question

The first task was distinguishing between a site-side technical failure and a Google algorithm update. The GA traffic graph was annotated directly using raw GA data — not third-party approximations — to identify the collapse date.

What the crawl found

A Screaming Frog technical crawl identified the cause. Multiple key content pages in the /cookers/ category were returning 403 Forbidden (Client Error) / Non-Indexable [Screaming Frog crawl, Feb 2022]:

  • /cookers/electric-cookers-induction-vs-ceramic-cooker-tops/
  • /cookers/how-to-buy-a-new-gas-cooker/
  • /cookers/range-cooker-guide/
  • /cookers/what-is-a-range-cooker/
  • /cookers/do-electric-cookers-come-with-plugs/
  • /cookers/what-are-the-different-range-cooker-sizes/
  • /cookers/does-le-creuset-work-with-induction-hobs/

One additional page had been incorrectly canonicalised. A secondary crawl filtered to image files found 523 image URLs in /wp-content/uploads/ also returning 403 Forbidden [Screaming Frog crawl, Feb 2022] — Content-Type was text/html, not image files, eliminating the site’s entire image SEO footprint simultaneously.

The outcome

Metric Pre-collapse (Nov 24–30, 2021) Post-collapse (Dec 8, 2021) Change
Top-3 keywords 68 5 −91% (64 lost)
Top-10 keywords 157 56 −64% (101 lost)
Organic visibility 9.60% 2.50% −7.74pp
Estimated daily traffic 72.66 24.84 −66%

[SEMrush Landscape, Nov 24–30 and Dec 8, 2021]1

347 keywords declined simultaneously in a two-week window against 14 improving [SEMrush, Dec 2021]1.

The recommendation — and what happened

Roll back to the prior URL structure to restore indexed paths, then rebuild the architecture correctly from a stable baseline. Lisa chose not to implement the rollback. The engagement concluded at the diagnostic stage.

What this demonstrates

Sequencing the diagnosis correctly — rule out algorithm event first using raw GA data before diagnosing a technical cause — is the correct method for any unexplained ranking collapse.

Sources

  1. SEMrush. Landscape report for whichkitchenappliance.com, Nov 24–30, 2021 and Dec 8, 2021. Keyword visibility, top-3/top-10/top-100 counts, average position, and estimated daily traffic on file. ↩︎ ↩︎ ↩︎

  2. Google Analytics. Session and user export for whichkitchenappliance.com, April 2021–January 2022. En-gb geographic segment. ↩︎
description: A Screaming Frog crawl identified 403 Forbidden errors on every high-ranking /cookers/ content page and 523 blocked images. The collapse wasn't an algorithm update — it was a URL restructure that broke indexed paths.
title: whichkitchenappliance.com: How a URL restructure generated 403s on every ranking page and collapsed a site overnight

content:
The two projects

Project 1 — Panic Away AdWords campaign. Panic Away was an established digital product in the anxiety and panic attack self-help category, sold through affiliate networks with commission rates sufficient to support paid traffic acquisition [ClickBank affiliate program, c. 2010]1.

Project 2 — Meditation software YouTube affiliate. Separately, YouTube was identified as an emerging channel for affiliate content in the adjacent wellness space.

What was evaluated before spending

For the AdWords campaign: the Panic Away affiliate offer, commission structure, and estimated CPC for anxiety and panic attack search queries in US metro markets [AdWords planner data, c. 2010]. The question was whether the margin between commission and ad spend could be made positive through targeted campaign execution. It could.

The results

AdWords: The campaign produced a positive return on ad spend — affiliate commissions exceeded advertising spend [AdWords campaign exports, engagement records]2.

YouTube: The video generated affiliate commission income over time through viewer conversions, creating a passive income stream from a single content asset [YouTube Analytics, engagement records]2.

What this demonstrates

Evaluating a conversion offer’s margin potential before committing ad budget is the step most operators skip. The AdWords campaign succeeded because the math was done first. The YouTube asset succeeded because a single piece of optimized content continued converting without additional spend.

Sources

  1. Panic Away. Affiliate program via ClickBank. panicaway.com. Product by Barry McDonagh; affiliate commissions paid through ClickBank network at time of campaign. ↩︎

  2. Production records. AdWords campaign performance exports and YouTube Analytics data on file. ↩︎ ↩︎
description: A self-directed Google AdWords campaign promoting Panic Away (anti-anxiety digital product) produced a positive return on ad spend without agency support. A separately produced YouTube video for a meditation software affiliate generated ongoing passive income from viewer conversions.
title: Panic Away: A self-funded AdWords campaign that returned profit, and a YouTube asset that kept paying after

content:
The opportunity

The search query where’s my cell phone generated substantial monthly search volume. Competitive research in 2013 found only two websites providing substantive solutions. The remainder of the SERP was thin, low-quality, or irrelevant. No established brand dominated the space.

The build

The site was built on WordPress under the author username Jon4822 with all content written in-house. Content architecture addressed the core user problem across four distinct H2 sections. Semantic optimization tools were applied. A PR5 anchor text backlink was acquired to reinforce the ranking position [engagement records, 2013].

The result

The site ranked in the top 3 organic positions, achieving the number-two position at peak [engagement records, 2013–2015].

Google AdSense generated weekly income for approximately 15 months from sustained organic traffic [engagement records].

What ended it

Google launched its native Find My Phone feature directly within search results. The site remained live from August 2013 through September 2017 — 27 captures documented [Internet Archive / Wayback Machine]1.

The lesson

Ranking an exact-match domain against two weak incumbents on a high-volume informational query is repeatable. The risk is platform dependency: when Google decides a query intent is better served by a SERP feature, third-party content loses.

Sources

  1. Internet Archive / Wayback Machine. wheresmycellphone.net. 27 captures documented between August 14, 2013 and September 12, 2017. web.archive.org. ↩︎
description: Phone misplacement is a near-universal recurring experience. In 2013, only two websites provided substantive solutions. The third was built — ranked to number two — and generated AdSense income for 15 months until Google absorbed the query with a native feature.
title: wheresmycellphone.net: How a universal frustration became a top-3 organic ranking and 15 months of AdSense income

content:
The situation

Daniel operated Cassie’s (cassies.com.au), an adult entertainment business across five Australian locations: New South Wales (Sydney), Victoria, and three additional states [client brief, Sep 2022]. He was ranking adequately for primary metropolitan targets but sought stronger visibility in smaller city and suburban markets.

Engagement: September 26, 2022.

The constraints

The adult industry classification created documented limitations [engagement records, Sep 2022]:

  • Citation directories: many declined to work with adult content businesses
  • Backlink vendors: same restriction applied
  • GMB verification: required exterior business signage photography — difficult for a business without a conventional retail storefront 1
  • Competitors: including search keywords directly in their GMB business name field in violation of Google’s guidelines2 — Google was not acting on reports despite them being filed

What the initial audit found

The GMB profile was not optimised — the description was too short and lacked keyword coverage [GBP audit, Sep 2022]. Facebook and LinkedIn business pages were both absent [social audit, Sep 2022]. BrightLocal citation management was already underway for Sydney [BrightLocal account, Sep 2022]3.

What the audit recommended

  • GMB description: Expand and optimise within category limitations
  • Facebook and LinkedIn business pages: Establish both
  • BrightLocal: Continue citation completion for Sydney 3
  • Competitor name-stuffing: Do not replicate — long-term enforcement risk outweighs short-term advantage 2
  • Secondary markets without a physical GBP listing: On-page location targeting and blog posts

What this demonstrates

Constraint mapping first — what’s available, what isn’t, and what can realistically be done within those limits — is the correct approach when standard playbook tools are unavailable.

Sources

  1. Google. Verify your Business Profile. support.google.com/business. Documents verification methods including postcard, phone, video, and exterior business signage requirement. ↩︎

  2. Google. Google Business Profile Name Guidelines. support.google.com/business. Explicitly prohibits including keywords, location names, or promotional information in the business name field. ↩︎ ↩︎

  3. BrightLocal. Citation Building and NAP Consistency Services. brightlocal.com. Citation management platform used for Sydney location. ↩︎ ↩︎
description: Five Australian locations. Citation directories that decline adult content. GMB verification requiring exterior signage photos. Competitors stuffing search keywords into their GMB business names in violation of Google's guidelines — and Google not acting on reports.
title: Cassie's: Local SEO for a 5-location adult entertainment business, with every standard playbook tool unavailable

content:
The window

In 2008, the electronic cigarette category was in its early commercial phase. Consumer awareness was growing, affiliate programs were emerging, but the review and comparison content landscape was almost entirely empty [Wayback Machine archive, 2008]1.

The build

The site was built on Weebly and structured as a multi-section affiliate review property [Wayback Machine archive]1. Navigation covered: HOME, E-CIG BLOG, ABOUT ELECTRONIC CIGARETTES, BUYING ELECTRONIC CIGARETTE BRANDS, ELECTRONIC CIGARETTE COUPONS, and a dedicated Greensmoke page.

Ten brands were reviewed: Greensmoke (featured and recommended), Vaporzone, Whitecloud, Premium E Cigarette, No Safe Cig, Volcano, eSmoke, VyCig, South Beach Smoke, and Blurip [site archive, 2008–2011]1. All on-site SEO was executed, submitted to online directories, and integrated third-party display advertising alongside affiliate links.

The result

Several dozen readers purchased electronic cigarette products through the affiliate links, with a portion becoming repeat buyers [engagement records]. Affiliate commission income was generated consistently for approximately 24 months [engagement records]. Third-party display advertising — including a Gold IRA Future banner visible in the Weebly archive — provided a secondary passive income layer [Wayback Machine archive]1.

Why it worked

Entry timing was the primary driver. The same site built two years later would not have achieved the same organic position or income duration. The early-adopter window closed as the category saturated.

Sources

  1. Internet Archive / Wayback Machine. electroniccigarettebrands.weebly.com. Site archive confirms copyright notice 2008–2011, multi-section navigation structure, and Greensmoke featured placement. web.archive.org. ↩︎ ↩︎ ↩︎ ↩︎
description: In 2008, the electronic cigarette review space was nearly empty. A structured affiliate review site built on Weebly covered ten brands and generated commission income for approximately 24 months before the niche saturated.
title: electroniccigarettebrands.weebly.com: 24 months of affiliate income from an empty niche and an early-adopter window

content:
The situation

SpideyGames.com was a free online games website monetized through Google AdSense. The site had grown from a clean two-URL structure to four URL types without a clear rationale [site audit, Mar 2022]. A 404 error page had been indexed by Google with a content-style page title. Meta descriptions had serious duplication issues across pages [site audit, Mar 2022].

Advisory date: 2022-07-29T12:30:00-05:00 27–29, 2020. Platform: Angular. Implementation responsibility: Harish.

What the audit mapped

Correct architecture [site audit, Mar 2022]:

  • Home page → game-type category pages → game info pages and game play pages
  • Utility pages as separate paths

Issues identified [site audit]:

  • Indexed 404 page with a content-style title — advised 301 redirect
  • Four URL types where two served the crawl purpose — advised consolidation
  • Meta description duplication — systemic

What the audit recommended

  • 301 redirect on the indexed 404 page
  • Shared the Google Quality Raters Guide — manual review criteria1
  • Provided an SEO content editor tool
  • Link strategy: audience-first partnerships with high-traffic gaming sites over backlink acquisition
  • Explicit flag: AdSense monetization requires UX as the primary design priority2

The outcome

Advisory delivered. Harish confirmed he had begun redirecting number-based URLs [engagement records, Mar 2020]. Angular implementation was Harish’s responsibility throughout.

Sources

  1. Google Search Central. Search Quality Evaluator Guidelines. Published guidance governing E-E-A-T assessment by Google’s human quality raters. ↩︎

  2. Google. AdSense Program Policies and UX Guidance. support.google.com/adsense. Documents the relationship between site UX quality and ad serving performance. ↩︎
description: An AdSense-monetized games site had grown from a clean two-URL-per-game structure to four URL types, an indexed 404 page, and site-wide meta description duplication. The audit mapped the correct architecture, delivered a 301 redirect strategy, and flagged the UX imperative for ad-monetized properties.
title: SpideyGames.com: Fixing an Angular games site's crawl architecture before it cost AdSense revenue

content:
The hypothesis

The 2010 FIFA World Cup was anticipated to generate a significant spike in search activity and a secondary spike in large-screen television sales. The opportunity: publish targeted content ahead of the peak demand window, establish a ranking position before competition intensified, and monetize through TV affiliate commissions.

The execution

A targeted Squidoo page was published, leveraging the platform’s domain authority to rank new content rapidly. The page was published before the tournament to establish ranking before search volume peaked, with content structured to address World Cup viewership intent and direct readers toward large-screen television affiliate links [engagement records, 2010]1.

What happened

Ranking: The strategy worked. The page achieved strong page rank and generated significant organic traffic as World Cup search volume rose [engagement records, 2010]1.

Conversion: The affiliate strategy did not succeed. Visitors arriving from World Cup search queries did not convert to television purchases at a meaningful rate [engagement records, 2010]1.

The disconnect: informational tournament intent is not transactional purchasing intent.

The recovery

The page had demonstrated page rank and documented traffic volume [engagement records]1. A buyer interested in acquiring an established, ranked Squidoo property recognised that value and purchased it.

What this demonstrates

A well-ranked content asset retains sale value independent of whether its original monetization hypothesis proves correct. Ranking success and conversion success are separate outcomes that require separate validation.

Sources

  1. Production records. Squidoo page performance and asset sale documented. Squidoo platform decommissioned 2014; archived content transferred to HubPages. ↩︎ ↩︎ ↩︎ ↩︎
description: A pre-event Squidoo page targeting World Cup 2010 search traffic achieved strong organic ranking and significant traffic. TV affiliate conversions failed — tournament search intent doesn't convert to TV purchases. The ranked asset was sold to a buyer who recognized its residual value.
title: FIFA 2010 on Squidoo: The ranking worked. The conversion hypothesis didn't. The page sold anyway.

content:
The headline

706 of 790 pages non-indexable — 89% of the site invisible to Google [Screaming Frog crawl, Oct 2019]. HTL Dental operates 6 locations across the Greater Toronto Area. The homepage was set to noindex,follow. Every page on the domain shared the same meta description: “Just another WordPress site” — the default WordPress placeholder, never replaced [crawl data, Oct 2019].

The locations

Six active clinic addresses at the time of audit [HTL Dental location data, Oct 2019]:

  • Front Street — 363 Front St W, Toronto ON M5V 3S8
  • Fort York — 129 Fort York Blvd, Toronto ON M5V 0K2
  • North York — 12 Park Home Ave, North York ON M2N 6L9
  • Fairview — 70 Forest Manor Rd #3, North York ON M2J 0A9
  • Richmond Hill — 328 Hwy 7 #23, Richmond Hill ON L4B 3P7
  • Markham — 32 South Unionville Ave, Markham ON L3R 1J5

Six GTA clinics. One digital footprint fighting itself.

The structural failures

Three failures compounding each other, each independently capable of destroying organic visibility [Screaming Frog crawl, Oct 2019]:

1. Homepage blocked by noindex. The canonical entry point for any domain authority flow was tagged meta name="robots" content="noindex,follow". Google was instructed not to index it [crawl data, Oct 2019]. Inlinks from the rest of the site (991 internal inlinks to the homepage) passed link equity into a page Google was told to ignore.

2. Default WordPress meta description, site-wide. “Just another WordPress site” appeared as the meta description across every crawled page [crawl data, Oct 2019]. This is the out-of-box WordPress placeholder. It was never replaced. Every SERP listing for every page would display an identical, generic, trust-destroying snippet.

3. Homepage H1: “ABOUT US.” The page Google treats as the authority signal for the domain brand led with a section header, not a commercial identity signal [crawl data, Oct 2019].

The bilingual fragmentation

243 pages carried non-ASCII title tags — Chinese-language service pages mixed into the same domain without hreflang, without language segmentation, without separate URL structure [crawl data, Oct 2019]. Examples from the crawl:

  • 种牙服务 – HTL DENTAL (dental implants in Mandarin)
  • 活动义齿 – 假牙 – HTL DENTAL (dentures in Mandarin)
  • Porcelain Veneers – HTL DENTAL (English, same site)

Google cannot reliably serve multilingual pages from a single domain without explicit language signals. The Chinese-language content was competing with English-language content for the same URLs, with no declared targeting signal directing either to its intended audience.

The sitemap proliferation

197+ sitemap XML files were generated and accessible under htldental.ca/?sitemapN.xml [crawl data, Oct 2019]. All redirecting (301) to HTTPS equivalents. The sitemap architecture was producing more redirect chains than indexable destinations.

The indexability math

Status Pages % of site
Indexable 84 10.6%
Non-indexable 706 89.4%
Total crawled 790 —

[Screaming Frog crawl, Oct 2019]

Of 84 indexable pages: 6 locations, no location-specific landing pages, no service pages with commercial keyword targeting. The indexable fraction of the site contained no architecture for multi-location organic acquisition.

The paid spend signal

Facebook ad projection data and spam link acquisition screenshots were present in the audit file [audit materials, Oct 2019]. A business spending on paid acquisition while its organic infrastructure has a sitewide noindex on the homepage is paying to fill a funnel with a missing top.

Key takeaways

The three failures here are independent and sequential — any one of them would have been costly alone. Together they compound: the noindex blocks authority consolidation; the default meta description destroys CTR on whatever pages do index; the bilingual fragmentation prevents the Chinese-language patient base from finding relevant pages even if they rank.

The irony is structural: 991 internal inlinks pointed at a page Google was told to ignore. The site’s own internal linking was performing correctly — the meta tag was overriding it.

Fix priority: (1) remove noindex from homepage, (2) replace default WordPress meta descriptions site-wide, (3) set homepage H1 to a branded commercial identifier, (4) add hreflang declarations for Chinese-language pages.

Sources

Crawl data from Screaming Frog SEO Spider, October 2019. Location data from HTL Dental location notes, October 2019.
description: A 790-page crawl of a 6-location Toronto dental DSO found 706 non-indexable pages — 89% of the site blocked from Google. The homepage carried a noindex tag. Every page shared the default WordPress meta description: 'Just another WordPress site.' The homepage H1 was 'ABOUT US.'
title: HTL Dental: How a 6-location Toronto dental chain made 89% of its website invisible to Google

content:
The situation

Teasdale Masonry — a stone masonry contractor based in Northumberland, UK — experienced a verified rankings drop and commissioned a full SEO audit [audit records, 2019]. The audit covered: a full site crawl (Screaming Frog), a second crawl of the primary competitor (jaspratt.co.uk), and a complete SERP competitive analysis for the target keyword.

The target keyword SERP

“Stone masons in Northumberland” — the primary commercial target. Top-20 SERP competitive analysis [Moz link metrics, 2019]:

Pos Site Type DA PA Backlinks
1 mybuilder.com Aggregator 62 32 138
2 paulfosterstonemason.co.uk Stonemason 8 24 64
3 thomsonlocal.com Directory 52 30 0
5 yell.com (stonemasons) Directory 74 37 0
6 yell.com (monumental masons) Directory 74 37 0
7 ratedpeople.com Aggregator 56 29 0
8 192.com Directory 65 32 0
9 theconstructioncentre.co.uk Directory 39 25 9
10 lambeth-stonework.com Stonemason 15 17 16
16 jaspratt.co.uk Stonemason 8 18 107
18 richmond-memorials.co.uk Memorial mason 17 28 965

[Moz competitive analysis export, 2019]

The signal hiding in the SERP

Position 2: paulfosterstonemason.co.uk — DA 8, 64 backlinks, an individual stonemason’s site — ranking ahead of directories with DA 52–74. This is the audit’s critical finding.

When a small trade contractor’s site with a domain authority of 8 holds position 2, the SERP is not dominated by pure authority. It is dominated by relevance and topical specificity. The aggregators (positions 1, 3, 5–9) rank on directory authority. The one small site ranking on page 1 ranks because Google trusts it as the genuine entity for the query.

The question becomes: why was Teasdale Masonry not in the same position as Paul Foster?

The crawl comparison

Two crawls were run: Teasdale’s own site and competitor jaspratt.co.uk [Screaming Frog, 2019]. jaspratt.co.uk sat at position 16 in the same SERP — a directly comparable small stonemason site. The dual-crawl methodology was designed to identify whether Teasdale’s drop was technical (crawlability, indexation, on-page signals) or authority-based (link gap).

The comparison target was not the high-DA aggregators — it was the nearest-relevance competitor actually visible in the SERP.

The rankings drop context

A screenshot of the rankings drop was captured in the audit file [audit records, 2019]. The file was labelled “team force rankings drop” — a reference to an algorithm update or competitive incursion, not a penalty. Rankings drops in niche local trades frequently originate from a competitor’s citation or link acquisition rather than from Google action against the site.

The aggregator problem

8 of the top 10 results for “stone masons in Northumberland” are directory platforms the individual contractor cannot outrank on authority [Moz data, 2019]. The realistic path for a small masonry contractor in this SERP:

  1. Get listed on every aggregator ranking above you. mybuilder.com (position 1), ratedpeople.com (position 7), 192.com (position 8), theconstructioncentre.co.uk (position 9) — these are the platforms serving your customers. Presence within them is not optional.
  2. Target the long tail the aggregators cannot own. “Northumberland dry stone walling,” “stone restoration Northumberland,” “listed building stone repairs” — queries with commercial intent where the aggregator’s category pages don’t exist.
  3. Study Paul Foster. DA 8, ranking #2. What his page does that Teasdale’s doesn’t is the single most actionable finding in this audit.

Key takeaways

The SERP structure here is not hostile — it is predictable. Aggregators dominate the head term. A niche-specific, entity-verified small contractor can hold a top-2 position. The evidence is in the same SERP. The audit task is to identify the gap between the client and the example.

A rankings drop in a niche local trade is almost always one of three things: a technical regression, a competitor’s citation gain, or a page-level relevance problem. The crawl data isolates which.

Sources

Moz competitive analysis export, 2019. Screaming Frog SEO Spider crawl data (teasdalemasonry.co.uk + jaspratt.co.uk), 2019.

content:
The engagement

Acryl Design Ltd. — Winnipeg plastic fabrication and custom signage company. Services: acrylic signs, coroplast signs, custom retail displays, wooden signs, outdoor LED signs, trade show displays. Full local SEO audit delivered July 25, 2019 [audit records].

The keyword landscape

Target keyword: “signs winnipeg” — 140 searches/month (Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada), KD 25, CPC $3.15 [KWFinder export, July 2019]. Secondary targets:

Keyword Vol/mo KD CPC
signs winnipeg 140 25 $3.15
sign companies winnipeg 90 42 $4.54
custom signs winnipeg 30 74 $2.44
outdoor signs winnipeg 10 64 $4.60

[KWFinder export, July 2019]

“Custom signs winnipeg” at KD 74 with only 30 monthly searches indicates an overly-competitive micro-niche. The correct acquisition target was the head term — low difficulty, highest volume, and the one being captured by aggregator directories.

The citation audit

98 citations audited across the full citation landscape [citation tracker, July 2019]:

Status Count
Active 32
Potential (unclaimed) 61
Duplicate 5
Total 98

5 duplicate listings across high-authority directories created NAP (Name, Address, Phone) inconsistency — a confirmed local ranking suppression factor [citation tracker, July 2019]. High-value unclaimed opportunities included LinkedIn (Very High citation value), Yelp.com (Very High), Salespider (Very High), and BBB-adjacent directories.

The competitor gap

Competitor analysis pulled from creatique.com — an Ontario-based acrylic/LED signs manufacturer ranking nationally for adjacent terms [competitor analysis, July 2019]. Notable: creatique.com held position 1 for “acrylic shop board” and position 5 for “marketing display boards” nationally, but their Winnipeg-specific citation footprint was weak. Local dominance was available for a business willing to build it.

The SERP problem

The core issue: aggregator directories (yellowpages.ca, yelp.ca, canpages.ca) were occupying the SERP for “signs winnipeg” because their domain authority (67–93) dwarfed any individual local business [citation data, July 2019]. Acryl Design had active listings on several of these, but the listings were under-optimized — minimal descriptions, incomplete categories, no photo assets.

The tactic available: build authority citations on the platforms already ranking, optimize the existing listings, and close the 61-site gap that was leaving the NAP footprint fragmented.

The deliverables

Full audit delivered July 25, 2019 [audit records]:

  • Citation tracker: 98-row status matrix with DA scores, citation value ratings, and competitor comparison columns
  • Citation matrix PDF: plastic fabrication category, reputation manager view
  • Google My Business audit: primary GBP health check
  • Local search audit PDF: full score summary
  • SWOT analysis: business and digital presence matrix
  • Master keyword research sheet: Winnipeg signs vertical + competitor terms
  • Backlink summary: HTML export

Key takeaways

A citation footprint with 5 active duplicates and 61 unclaimed opportunities is not a minor gap — it is a fragmented NAP record that tells Google’s local algorithm different things about the same business. Every inconsistency is a confidence deduction.

The actionable path for a business in this position: close the duplicate gap first (suppression fix), claim and optimize the 10 highest-DA unclaimed citations next, then move down the value ladder. The directory aggregators ranking above you are not the competition — they are the platform. Get on them correctly.

Sources

KWFinder keyword data, July 2019. Citation tracker and citation matrix: Whitespark/manual audit, July 2019. Competitor analysis: SEMrush export, July 2019.
description: A full local SEO audit of a Winnipeg plastic fabrication and custom signage company found 32 active citations, 5 duplicates creating NAP conflicts, and 61 confirmed citation opportunities the business had never claimed. The primary target keyword — 'signs winnipeg' (140/mo, KD 25) — was being captured by aggregator directories the client didn't control.
title: Acryl Design: The Winnipeg signs company outranked by directories it didn't know existed
